California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
January 18, 1991
Anne M. Russell
Diehl & Rodewald
Attorneys at Law
10■■ Pacific Street
P.0. BOX 
■207
San Luis ObiSpo′  CA  93406
Re:  Your Request fOr lnfOrmal Assistance
Our Flle NO. I-90-46■
Dear Ms. Russell:
You have requeSted adv■
ce on beha■f of the Santa Mar■
a Pub■ic

sions of the P01itical Reform Act

OUESTIONS
■.  If the district enters into an exclusive 
■isting agree―
ment with a real estate broker for the sa■
e Or leaSe of district
property′  is the real estate broker considered a t'COnSu■
tanti=
under the Act?

000-9■0■5.  All Statutory references
are to the Covernment Code un■
ess otherwise indicatedo  COlllnission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regu■
ations Section
■8000′  et seq.  Al■ references to regulatiOns are to Title 2′
Division 6 0f the California Code of RegulatiOns.
2  .nformal asSiStance does not prov■
de the requestOr w■
th the
immunity proVided by an oPinion 3r forma■ Written adviCe.
(Government COde Section 831■
4, 2 Cal. COde of Regs.
section 
■8329(c)(3)。
)
428 J StrcCt,Suite 800 ● P.0.Box 807 0 Sacramento CA 95804‐0807 0 (916)322・5660


Our File No. A-90-46■
Page 2
2. Assurning the broker is a consultant, under vrhat
circurnstances is the broker deemed to be participating in the
making of a governnental decision under the Act?
3. Would district 
staff involvernent in the decision-naking
process provide t'significant intervening substantive reviewrr of
Lhe broklr's recommendations (i.e. to accePt an offer, enter into
a transaction) so as to insulate the broker from participating in
making a governmental decision and to exempt the broker from the
dj,scf osure and disquali fication provisions of the Act?
CONCLUS IONS
1. If the district 
enters into an exclusive listing
agreement with a real estate broker for the sale or lease of
district property, such broker is deerned a consultant under the
Act if he or she provides information, advice, recommendation or
counsel to the diatrict, 
is subject to the district's 
control and
direction, or possesses authority with respect to any district
decisions. If such broker acts indePendently of the district's
control and direction, and possesses no authority with respect to
any district 
decision beyond the rendition of infornation, advice,
relornmendation or counsel, the broker is not a consultant under
the Act.
2. 
We do not have sufficient information about the role of
such broker to reach a conclusion with respect to this question'
Holrever, if the broker negotiates with a ggvernmental entity or
private person or advises or makes recommendations to the
-decisionlmaker, without significant substantive review, the broker
is participating in the makinq of a governmental decision.
3. If the decision-making structure of the district 
provides
for an independent substanti,ve revj'ew and analysis by the district
staff or city rnanager, the broker/exclusive agent may not be
participating in rnaking a governmental decision under the Act.
uowever, this is interPreted very narrowly and we do not have
sufficient facts to reich a conclusion with respect to this
question. rf the broker's expertise is relied upon by the
district, 
the broker is considered a consultant.
FACTS
The district 
recently received unsolicited requests from
three 1oca1 real estate brokers, each proposing to act as the
exclusive agent for the district 
in the marketing, se11ing, and
Leasj-ng of district property_. In reviewing the proposals,
concerns arose about the potentj-aI for a conflict of interest due
to some of the brokers' dual roles as brokers/developers with
regard to property in the vicinity of the airport. 
Any broker
selected by the district 
would most likely be representing other
clients having property for sale or lease in the vicinity of the
airport. 
The broker may be naking recommendations to the Board as
to property to be sold or leased and possibly analyzing any offer
whj.ch is brought to the district. 
A proposed narketing progran to


Our File No. A-90-46■
Page 3
be paid for jointly by the district 
and.the broker would most
fiXify aia in the laIe of other properties listed with or owned by
the broker.
A. District Propertv Involved
The santa Maria Pub1ic Airport District currently has several
parcels of land for sale in an existing industrial park. The
aistrict 
also has prepared a specific plan for devel'opment of a
738 acre research park, with plans to develop the park and lease
the property on a long terur basis. fn addition, the district
leases substantial portions of its inproved property, such as
hangars, buildings, Parking areas, .ramP areas, and inactive
runiays, as !ret1 as most of its uninproved land for agricultural
and grazing purposes.
B. District's 
current Policies and Procedures
At present, the district 
has adopted a policy to pay a com-
rnission -for the sale of district 
property or the procurement of a
long-term (twenty years or more) lease. District property cur-
r".,il,y for sale is not listed rrith any specific broker' .Any
brokei or interested individual nay subrnit an offer on district
property to the district's 
general nanager. The general manager
iev-iews- the offer and may either subnit the offer directly to the
board, or may enter into negotiations with the party naking- the
offer. 
If a broker is involved in the transaction, the broker
usualLy participates in the negotiations. - The district 
counsel
review-s Lne otfer and nay draft a purchase agreement or lease'
Additional negotiations concerning the langnrage of the purchase
agreement or lehse nay involve the broker. The general.manager
.iy o. may not make a recommendation on the proposal which is
ullinateIy presented to the board. The broker is usually
available to anshrer questions.
Any broker who is oPerating under the current situation has
neither an on-going rel.ationship nor a contractual relationship
with the distrj.ct (other than the district's 
unilateral offer of
payment of a cornmission contained in its written policies, which
usually are incorporated into the final written agreenent).
If the district 
hires a broker as an exclusive agent, the
relationship will be contractual and on-going (for the period of
time set forth in the exclusive listing aqreemen!). Although the
ultimate decision to seII or lease a piece of property is nade by
the board, a broker with an on-going relationship with the board
nay be caLled upon to make recommendations on specific properties
or specific areas of the airport.
ANALYSTS
Section 87100 prohibits any public official 
from making,
participating in naking, or in any r{ray using his or her official


Our Fi■e No. A-90-46■
Page 4
position to influence a governmental decision in which the of-
iiciat 
has a financial interest. 
ttPublic officialtr is defined in
Section 82048 to include every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local government agency.
Regulation 18700(a) defines public official 
and consultant as
f o 1I o\.rs :
(a) "Pubfic official 
at any level of state or
1ocal governmentrr means every natural person who is
a member, officer, 
employee or consultant of a
state or loca1 government agency.
(2) "Consultanttr shall include any
natural person who provides, under contract,
information, advice, recommendation or counsel
to a state or loca1 government alrency,
provided, however, that "consultantrr shall not
include a person who:
' 
(A) conducts research and arrives
at conclusions with respect to his or her
rendition of information, advice, recom-
mendation or counsel independent of the
control and direction of the agency or of
any agency official, 
other than nornal
contract nonitoring i and
(B) Possesses no authority with
respect to any agency decision beyond the
rendition of information, advice, recon-
rnendation or counsel.
The broker/exclusive agent is considered a consultant if he
or she provides information, advice, recommendation or counsel to
the district, 
is subject to the district's 
control and direction,
or possesses authority with respect to any district 
decisions.
(Havden Advice Letter, No. A-84-319, copy enclosed. ) Conversely,
if the broker acts independent of the district's 
control and
di.recti.on, and possesses no authority with respect to any district
decision beyond the rendition of information, advice, recomnenda-
tion of counsel, the broker is not considered a consultant.
(Regulation 18700(a) (2); Havden Advice Letter, suprai Rose Advice
Letter, No. A-84-306; and Kaplan Advice Letter, No. A-82-1O8,
copies enclosed. )
when a person is retained to deliver a specific finished
product or to just provide infornation upon which the governing
body nay act, he or she would normally not be considered a
consultant. Therefore, a broker under the district,s 
current
policy, who does nothing more than present an offer to the
dj.strict, e/ould not be a consultant. Likewise, if the district
contracts with an exclusive broker who onlv presents an offer to
the district, 
the broker vould not fe consialred a consultant.


Our Fi■e NO. A-90-46■
Page 5
If the broker provides advice or counsel or recommends a
specific course of action on a continuing basis, he or she is
deemed to be a public official- under the Act. According to the
facts provided, if the district hires a broker/exclusive agent, he
or she would act as the exclusive agent for the district 
in the
marketing, selling or leasing of district property. In this
capacity, the broker lrould be providing inforrnation, advice,
recornmendation or counsel to the district 
regarding property to be
sold or leased. The broker nay also analyze offers brought to the
district, 
participate in negotiations and make recornmendations
regarding prices and property values. In this situation, the
broker would be deemed a consultant under the Act.
A broker/exclusive agent who is deemed a consultant is
subject to both the disclosure and disqual ification provisions of
the Act. The broker must be designated in the district's 
conflict
of interest code under a consultant category. As we previously
discussed, the district's 
current conflict of interest code does
not have a consul-tant category. The code nust be anended if a
broker/excfu.sive agent, who qualifies as a consultant under the
Act, is retained.
Participate in Makinq a Governmental Decisi.on
Pursuant to Regulation 18700(c), a publi,c official 
or
designated employee trparticipates in the naking of a governmental
decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her posi-
tion, he or she:
(1) Negotiates, without significant substan-
tive review, with a governmental entity or private
person regarding the decision; or
(2) Advises or makes recoEmendations to the
decision-maker, either directly or without
significant intervening substantive review, by:
(A) Conducting research or naking any
investigation which requires the exercise of
judgment on the part of the official 
or
designated enployee and the purpose of which
is to inftuence the decisionr. or
(B) Preparing or presenting any report,
analysis or opi.nion, orally or in rrriting,
which requires the exercise of judgnnent on the
part of the official 
or designated employee
and the purpose of which is to influence the
deci s ion .
Assumedly, the broker will be providing information, advice,
recommendation or counsel to the ai,strict with ,"=p."t [o oirers.
Ho$rever, if there is "significant intervening subsi,antive ieview,'


Our Fi■e No. A-90-46■
Page 6
of the broker's reconmendations, the broker hlill- not be
participating in a governmental- decision. (Regulation 18700(c);
Leidigh Advice Letter, No. A-89-320, copy enclosed.)
Hosrever, the Conmission has interpreted this exception very
narrowl,y. In Kaplan, suDra, lre stated that:
A consultant participates in a decision, even
if it is reviewed by severat of his superiors, i-f
those superiors rely on the data or analysis
prepared by the consultant e/ithout checkinq it
ind-pendently, if they rely on the professional
judgment of the consultant, or if the consultant in
sone other way actually nay influence the final
decision.
You have not provided us with sufficient inforrnation about
the refationship between the broker and the district 
staff or
general manager to determine if there is - significant intervening
iubstantive ieview. However, under our interpretation, if the
district 
staff or general nanager makes a decision as to a
potential lease or sale of district property, and relies upon the
Lroker's information or expertise, the broker is deened to be
participating in a governtnental decision.
Disoua 1i f ication
As a consultant of the district, 
the broker/exclusive agent
is a public official 
who is subject to the- provisions 
_ of the Act-
The A;t prohibits a public officiat 
frour uraking, participating in,
or using his or. her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which he or she knolrs or has reason to know of a
f j.nancial interest. 
(section 87100.) A public official 
has a
f j.nancial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decisj.on will have a rnaterial financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public general.ly, on the
official 
or a member of his immediate faniLy or on:
(a) Any'business entity in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect investment rrorth
one thousand dollars (91000) or more.
(b) Any real property in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars (S1000) or more.
(c) Any source of incone, other than gifts
and other than loans by'a cornmercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms avaiLable to the public without regard to
official 
status, aggregating two hundred fifty 
dol-
lars ($250) or more in value provided to, received


Our Fi■e No. A-90-46■
Page 7
by or promised to the public official Irithin 12
months prior to the time when the decision is rnade.
(d) Any business entity in rrhich the public
official 
is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.
(e) Any donor of, or any interruediary or
agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty do]Iars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by, or prornised to the public
official 
within 12 nonths prior to the time when
the decision is made.
section 87103 (a) -(e) .
As a public official, 
the broker may not participate in a
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect on any of his or her econornic
interests. 
(section 87103(a)-(e).) This is a separate analysis
which can be-done on a case-by-case basis when the appropriate
facts are provided.
I trust that this letter has answered your questions. If you
have any further questions regardinq this natter, please contact
ne at (916) 322-59OL.
S incerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General counsel

CONCLUSION: S
1. If the district 
enters into an exclusive listing
agreement with a real estate broker for the sale or lease of
district property, such broker is deerned a consultant under the
Act if he or she provides information, advice, recommendation or
counsel to the diatrict, 
is subject to the district's 
control and
direction, or possesses authority with respect to any district
decisions. If such broker acts indePendently of the district's
control and direction, and possesses no authority with respect to
any district 
decision beyond the rendition of infornation, advice,
relornmendation or counsel, the broker is not a consultant under
the Act.
2. 
We do not have sufficient information about the role of
such broker to reach a conclusion with respect to this question'
Holrever, if the broker negotiates with a ggvernmental entity or
private person or advises or makes recommendations to the
-decisionlmaker, without significant substantive review, the broker
is participating in the makinq of a governmental decision.
3. If the decision-making structure of the district 
provides
for an independent substanti,ve revj'ew and analysis by the district
staff or city rnanager, the broker/exclusive agent may not be
participating in rnaking a governmental decision under the Act.
uowever, this is interPreted very narrowly and we do not have

district, 
the broker is considered a consultant.
